questions.question: Should WebSocket endpoints (ws/markets/{id}/prices, ws/markets/{id}/events, ws/accounts/{id}/risk) be included in the OpenAPI spec, or only REST endpoints?
questions.why_it_matters: OpenAPI 3.0 doesn't natively support WebSocket documentation. Including them requires AsyncAPI or custom x-extensions, which adds complexity.

questions.question: Should the spec include the legacy health endpoints (/health, /healthz) or only the versioned /v1/ routes?
questions.why_it_matters: Including legacy endpoints clutters the spec; excluding them may hide important operational endpoints that monitoring tools depend on.

questions.question: What OpenAPI version to target - 3.0.3 (widest tooling support) or 3.1.0 (JSON Schema alignment)?
questions.why_it_matters: 3.1.0 offers better JSON Schema compatibility but some Swagger UI versions and code generators don't fully support it yet.

questions.question: Should the spec document the conditional auth behavior (BAYES_REQUIRE_AGENT_ID=true/false) or assume auth is always required for protected routes?
questions.why_it_matters: The auth requirement is configurable via env var. Documenting both states adds complexity; documenting only 'required' is simpler but may mislead development deployments.

questions.question: Are the existing contract-freeze docs in docs/ (t586, t1737, etc.) the canonical source of truth for response schemas, or should I derive schemas purely from server.py code?
questions.why_it_matters: If contract docs and code diverge, I need to know which to trust for accurate schema definitions in the OpenAPI spec.

assumptions.assumption: Target OpenAPI 3.0.3 for maximum tooling compatibility
assumptions.if_wrong: Would need to restructure some schema definitions for 3.1.0 JSON Schema alignment

assumptions.assumption: WebSocket endpoints should be excluded from the OpenAPI spec (REST-only focus)
assumptions.if_wrong: Would need to add AsyncAPI spec or use x-extensions for WS documentation

assumptions.assumption: Document auth as required on protected routes (the production-expected behavior)
assumptions.if_wrong: Would need to add conditional security schemes or document both modes

assumptions.assumption: The server.py code is the source of truth for schemas (contract docs may be stale)
assumptions.if_wrong: May produce schemas that don't match intended contract guarantees

assumptions.assumption: Include all endpoints (legacy health + versioned) in a single spec file
assumptions.if_wrong: Would split into separate specs or omit legacy endpoints

assumptions.assumption: Output file is docs/openapi.yaml as specified in the task description

decomposition_assessment.complexity_reason: Single output file (openapi.yaml) documenting ~25 endpoints from one source file (server.py). No architectural changes, no code modifications - purely documentation generation. However, server.py is 5500+ lines with complex request/response shapes.
decomposition_assessment.uncertainty_reason: Endpoints and schemas are well-defined in code and existing contract docs. The main unknowns are preference decisions (OpenAPI version, scope of what to include) rather than technical unknowns.
independent_subproblems: Document auth scheme and rate limiting
decomposition_assessment.decision_reason: Complexity 3 and uncertainty 2 are below spawn thresholds. While there are logically separable endpoint groups, they all share common schemas (error responses, meta envelope, auth) and must be coherent in a single YAML file. Parallelizing would create merge conflicts in the shared spec.
